glossary.definition: An older style of AI that simply acts like a giant auto-complete, continuing whatever text you give it without understanding conversation.
A modern AI designed specifically to act as a conversational partner, understanding when it is its turn to speak and how to be helpful.
Stands for Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback, a training method used to teach AI models how to behave like helpful, harmless assistants.

mental_model.intuition: A completion model is an auto-complete engine. A chat model is an auto-complete engine that was fine-tuned to play a conversation partner.
mental_model.description: At their core, both model types do the same thing: predict the most likely next token given everything that came before. The difference is what they were trained to continue. A base completion model continues any text as if it were the middle of a document. A chat model was fine-tuned (with RLHF) on conversation transcripts, so it's optimised to continue the 'assistant' turn of a structured dialogue — it knows how conversations end.

sections.body: Base (completion) models like the original GPT-3 took a single string and continued it. If you asked 'What is the capital of France?', it would output '...Paris, which is also known as...' — a document continuation, not a clean answer. To get answers out, you had to prompt-engineer carefully: 'Q: What is the capital of France? A:'. The model then continued with 'Paris'.
sections.recap: Completion models: raw text in, text continuation out. No conversation structure.

sections.body: Chat models (gpt-4o, claude-3-5-sonnet, etc.) were fine-tuned on dialogue data using Reinforcement Learning from Human Feedback (RLHF). They learned what a helpful, harmless reply looks like in a conversation. They natively understand the system/user/assistant role structure and generate proper conversational replies — not raw continuations. You send messages[]; they reply as the assistant.
sections.recap: Chat models: trained to be a helpful conversational partner, not just a text predictor.

sections.body: From an API perspective, the shift from completions to chat completions is structural. The older /v1/completions endpoint took a single prompt string. The modern /v1/chat/completions endpoint takes a messages[] list. OpenAI has been deprecating the pure completion endpoint for its flagship models — for new projects, always use the chat completions endpoint, even for non-conversational tasks.
sections.recap: Use /chat/completions for everything — even single-turn, non-conversational tasks.

sections.body: For single-turn non-conversational tasks (summarize this document, classify this label), the difference is thin: put the task in the user message. For multi-turn conversation, the chat structure is essential: the model maintains its persona and understands turn-taking. For fine-tuning and building agents, chat models are the standard — almost all modern tooling assumes the chat format.
sections.recap: New projects → chat completions API, regardless of whether the task is 'conversational'.

code_snippets.title: Legacy completion endpoint (avoid for new projects)
code_snippets.code:
# DO NOT USE for new projects — endpoint deprecated for flagship models
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

# Old way: single string, model continues it
response = client.completions.create(
    model='gpt-3.5-turbo-instruct',  # one of the few models that still supports this
    prompt='The capital of France is',
    max_tokens=5,
)
print(response.choices[0].text)   # ' Paris.'
code_snippets.explanation: Shown for context only. The /v1/completions endpoint is deprecated for GPT-4 and later models. If you're reading old blog posts that use this shape, translate them to the chat format below.

code_snippets.title: Modern chat completions endpoint (use this)
code_snippets.code:
from openai import OpenAI
client = OpenAI()

# New way: structured messages[], works for chat and non-chat tasks alike
response = client.chat.completions.create(
    model='gpt-4o',
    messages=[
        {'role': 'system', 'content': 'Answer factually and concisely.'},
        {'role': 'user',   'content': 'What is the capital of France?'},
    ],
)
print(response.choices[0].message.content)   # 'Paris.'
code_snippets.explanation: Even for a one-shot factual question, use the chat format. It gives you the system message lever, consistent structure across all tasks, and access to all the latest model features (tools, structured output, vision).

common_mistakes.title: Using the completion endpoint for new projects
common_mistakes.explanation: The /v1/completions endpoint is deprecated for GPT-4+ models. It lacks access to tools, structured output, and latest model versions. All new projects should use /v1/chat/completions.

common_mistakes.title: Thinking the chat format only applies to chatbots
common_mistakes.explanation: The chat format is the standard interface for ALL tasks — classification, summarization, extraction, agents. The 'chat' name is misleading; it just means 'structured message list', not 'must be a chatbot'.

recall_questions.q: What is the fundamental difference between a base completion model and a chat model?
recall_questions.answer: Base completion models predict text continuations from a raw string. Chat models are fine-tuned on conversation data (RLHF) to generate helpful, structured conversational replies from a messages[] list.

recall_questions.q: Which API endpoint should you use for all new projects — /v1/completions or /v1/chat/completions?
recall_questions.answer: /v1/chat/completions — the completions endpoint is deprecated for flagship models and lacks access to tools, structured output, and latest model features.

recall_questions.q: Can you use the chat completions API for non-conversational tasks like document summarization?
recall_questions.answer: Yes — the chat format is the standard interface for ALL tasks. Put instructions in the system message and the document in the user message. The 'chat' name is misleading; it just means 'structured message list'.

oa_questions.question: A tutorial from 2021 uses openai.Completion.create() with a raw prompt string. You're adapting it for gpt-4o. What changes?
oa_questions.answer: Switch from client.completions.create(prompt='...') to client.chat.completions.create(messages=[...]). Move the instructions into a system message and the task input into a user message. The response field also changes: response.choices[0].text → response.choices[0].message.content.
oa_questions.approach: This is a direct API translation question. The key changes are endpoint, input shape (string→messages list), and output field.

oa_questions.question: You need to build a document-classification pipeline that labels support tickets into categories. Should you use the chat completions API or completions API? Why?
oa_questions.answer: Chat completions API. Even though this isn't a conversational task, the chat format is the modern standard for all tasks. Put the classification instructions in the system message, the ticket text in the user message. The completions endpoint is deprecated for flagship models and doesn't support tools, structured output, or the latest model versions.
oa_questions.approach: Reinforce that 'chat' doesn't mean conversational — it's just the API format for all GPT-4-class tasks.
